retrieved_docs.title: Metronidazole -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of action of metronidazole occurs through a four-step process. Step one is the entry into the organism by diffusion across the cell membranes of anaerobic and aerobic pathogens. However, antimicrobial effects are limited to anaerobes. [6] Step two involves reductive activation by intracellular transport proteins by altering the chemical structure of pyruvate-ferredoxin oxidoreductase. The reduction of metronidazole creates a concentration gradient in the cell that drives uptake of more drugs and promotes free radical formation that is cytotoxic. [7] Step three, interactions with intracellular targets, is achieved by cytotoxic particles interacting with host cell DNA resulting in DNA strand breakage and fatal destabilization of the DNA helix. [8] Step four is the breakdown of cytotoxic products. Metronidazole is also cytotoxic to facultatively anaerobic bacteria like Helicobacter pylori and Gardnerella vaginalis , but the mechanism of action to these pathogens is not well understood. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Metronidazole -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Metronidazole diffuses into the organism, inhibits protein synthesis by interacting with DNA, and causes a loss of helical DNA structure and strand breakage. Therefore, it causes cell death in susceptible organisms.

retrieved_docs.title: Metronidazole -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Metronidazole has rapid bactericidal effects against anaerobic bacteria with a killing rate proportional to the drug concentration. Concentration-dependent bactericidal properties have been demonstrated against Entamoeba histolytica and T richomonas vaginalis . [3] [4] Furthermore, it kills Bacteroides fragilis, and Clostridium perfringens more rapidly than treatment doses of clindamycin. It also penetrates the blood-brain barrier. [5]

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Antiparasitic Drugs -- Mechanism of Action -- Antiprotozoal Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Metronidazole and tinidazole are 5-nitroimidazole derivatives. Metronidazole has both antibacterial and antiprotozoal properties. It is active against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases such as trichomoniasis and giardiasis. Within the parasites, it engages in redox reactions, releasing toxic reactive intermediates, making it effective as a luminal and extra-luminal amoebicide. The metronidazole metabolites are taken up by the deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA), leading to the DNA disruption and, consequently, protein synthesis inhibition. Tinidazole has a similar structure to metronidazole and shares a similar activity profile. However, tinidazole has a higher cure rate for protozoal infections and a better toxicity profile than metronidazole.

retrieved_docs.title: Metronidazole -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the indications to begin metronidazole therapy. Outline dosing of metronidazole for some of the more common indications. Describe the importance of antibiotic stewardship when initiating a broad-spectrum antimicrobial like metronidazole. Explain the importance of antibiotic stewardship and how it affects antimicrobial selection as it pertains to improving care coordination among the interprofessional team when initiating antibiotic therapy with metronidazole. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Metronidazole -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Metronidazole is one of the mainstay drugs for the treatment of anaerobic bacterial infections, protozoal infections, and microaerophilic bacterial infections. It is cytotoxic to facultative anaerobic microorganisms.

retrieved_docs.title: Metronidazole -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Metronidazole is one of the mainstay drugs for the treatment of anaerobic bacterial infections, protozoal infections, and microaerophilic bacterial infections. It is cytotoxic to facultative anaerobic microorganisms. It is used to treat intestinal amebiases, liver amebiasis, bacterial septicemia, bone and joint infections, meningitis, brain abscess, endocarditis, endometritis, bacterial vaginosis, intra-abdominal infections, lower respiratory tract infections, skin structure infections, and surgical prophylaxis colorectal surgeries. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, contraindications, monitoring, and other key factors pertinent to interprofessional team members in the care of patients being treated with this medication.

retrieved_docs.title: Metronidazole -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Topical metronidazole is indicated for rosacea. It is used intravaginally for bacterial vaginosis

retrieved_docs.title: Antiparasitic Drugs -- Mechanism of Action -- Antiprotozoal Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Antigiardial agents [14] : Metronidazole - The first choice of medication for giardia is metronidazole (see antiamoebic agents). Furazolidone is a nitrofuran compound with antibacterial and antiprotozoal activity. It is an alternative to metronidazole and like metronidazole, undergoes a reduction in the trophozoite, releasing toxic reactive intermediates. Its use is encouraged among the pediatric population as it is available as a liquid suspension. Tinidazole – see antiamoebic agents. Albendazole – see anticestodal drugs. Trypanocidal agents:

retrieved_docs.title: Anaerobic Infections -- Treatment / Management -- Step 2
retrieved_docs.content: Metronidazole : Has excellent activity against Gram-negatives such as Bacteroides fragilis . Its activity against Gram positives is good, although less reliable. Metronidazole has excellent bioavailability (100%), and it penetrates well into the tissue, including the central nervous system and abdominal cavity. Metronidazole gives a metallic taste in the mouth, which is frequently cited as the reason for discontinuation.

retrieved_docs.title: Metronidazole -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Dosing for bacterial vaginosis: [12] Oral: 500 mg twice each day for seven days.

retrieved_docs.title: Metronidazole -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Metronidazole is contraindicated in patients with documented hypersensitivity to the drug or its components, and it should be avoided in first-trimester pregnancy. Patients should also avoid consuming alcohol or products containing propylene glycol while taking metronidazole and within three days of therapy completion. Metronidazole is likewise contraindicated if there has been recent disulfiram use within the past two weeks. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Metronidazole -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: There should be judicious prescribing of metronidazole by providers only for known indications and high clinical suspicion for needing treatment of anaerobic bacterial infections, protozoal infections, and microaerophilic bacterial infections. Overprescribing may contribute to increasing future antibiotic resistance to the drug, so a pharmacy consult is necessary for the prescriber to ensure directed therapy. The documentation already exists regarding increased resistance to metronidazole in the treatment of C. difficile infections. Also, prescribers, nurses, and pharmacists should routinely educate patients to abstain from alcoholic beverages while taking metronidazole, which will help lead to fewer disulfiram reaction symptoms; nursing will play a role in this monitoring and for other potential adverse effects. The patient should also be informed by the health care provider of the possible change in urine color while on this medicine. The team of health professionals, including physicians, physician assistants, nurses, and pharmacists, must work together to provide the best care for these patients when using metronidazole in any formulation. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Metronidazole -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Dosing for rosacea with 1% gel or cream daily, or 0.75% cream or lotion twice a day.

retrieved_docs.title: Metronidazole -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Dosing for intra-abdominal infections: [14] Oral, IV: 500 mg every 8 hours as in an appropriate combination regimen. Therapy duration is 4 to 7 days following adequate source control, and a longer duration is necessary for uncomplicated appendicitis and diverticulitis managed nonoperatively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1380
retrieved_docs.content: Tetracycline and its derivatives formulated as topical antibiotics are classified within the tetracyklin group, indicating that they share the characteristic chemical structure and antimicrobial properties of tetracycline agents. Similarly, the combination regimen consisting of bismuth subcitrate, metronidazole, and tetracycline is also considered part of the tetracyklin class, reflecting its inclusion of a tetracycline component that contributes to the overall therapeutic effect. Both the topical antibiotic preparations and the bismuth‑subcitrate/metronidazole/tetracycline combination are therefore linked by their membership in the tetracyklin family, underscoring their related mechanisms of action and relevance in antimicrobial treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: Metronidazole is a nitroimidazole derivative that functions as an antibacterial, antiprotozoal and anti‑infective agent and is classified under multiple pharmacological groups, including Imidazole derivatives antibacterial for systemic use, Imidazole derivatives gynecological anti‑infectives, Nitroimidazole derivatives antiprotozoal agents (effective against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases), and local anti‑infective agents. Within the broader hierarchy of antiparasitic agents, metronidazole appears as a child concept, underscoring its role in treating parasitic infections, and it is a component of the combination regimen bismuth subcitrate / metronidazole / tetracycline used for gastrointestinal infections. Metronidazole shares broader therapeutic links with ADH antagonists and furazolidone, the latter being classified as a local anti‑infective through an inverse‑ISA relationship. The class “Medicinal product categorized by disposition” is a parent category that includes

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1538
retrieved_docs.content: Imidazole derivatives that are antibacterial for systemic use comprise several specific agents, including metronidazole, ornidazole, and tinidazole. Both metronidazole and ornidazole are also classified as antiparasitic agents, indicating that these drugs have activity against parasites in addition to their systemic antibacterial properties. Metronidazole is further linked to local anti‑infective agents, suggesting its relevance in topical or localized infection treatment. This interconnected classification highlights the multifunctional role of metronidazole and ornidazole within the broader groups of systemic imidazole antibacterials and antiparasitic medications, while tinidazole remains a member of the systemic imidazole antibacterial category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: 3Mix ointment is linked to the antimicrobial agent metronidazole, indicating that the ointment formulation contains or is equivalent to metronidazole for topical use. Additionally, ADH antagonists are described as having a broader relationship with metronidazole, suggesting that metronidazole may share pharmacological or therapeutic pathways with agents that antagonize antidiuretic hormone, or that it is considered within the larger class of drugs influencing ADH‑related mechanisms. Together, these connections highlight the role of metronidazole both as a component of combination topical preparations such as 3Mix ointment and as a drug that may intersect with the broader pharmacology of ADH antagonists.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1605
retrieved_docs.content: ADH antagonists represent a broad pharmacologic class that includes both metronidazole and furazolidon. Metronidazole is associated with local anti‑infective agents, indicating its use as a topical antimicrobial. Furazolidon is likewise classified as a local anti‑infective agent, making it a specific member of that group. Thus, both metronidazole and furazolidon function as antimicrobial agents within the wider ADH antagonist category and are employed in the treatment of localized infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: Metronidazole is an imidazole‑derived drug that belongs to several subclasses, including systemic antibacterial agents, gynecological anti‑infectives, nitroimidazole antiprotozoal agents for amoebiasis, and local anti‑infective preparations, and it is classified as a child of antiparasitic agents. It shares the “Medicinal product categorized by disposition” grouping with penicillin‑sensitive penicillins, benzodiazepine antagonists, and ion‑exchange resins such as colestipol hydrochloride, an anionic bile‑acid sequestrant used to lower cholesterol. ADH antagonists overlap pharmacologically with metronidazole and furazolidone, although furazolidone is not listed among local anti‑infective agents. Imidazole and triazole derivatives comprise a large pharmacological group of topical antifungals—terconazole, butoconazole, fenticonazole, isoconazole, ornidazole, oxiconazole, sertaconazole, tioconazole, flutrimazole, omoconazole, efinaconazole, and ekonazole—most of which are also classified

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 117
retrieved_docs.content: guides diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic management. Collectively, the knowledge network positions metronidazole and its related nitroimidazole and imidazole derivatives at the core of antibacterial, antiprotozoal and local anti‑infective therapy, while the disposition‑based framework integrates ADH antagonists, benzodiazepine antagonists, a wide spectrum of antifungal imidazole/triazole agents, ion‑ and anion‑exchange compounds, specific penicillin derivatives, and antitrichomonal agents such as furazolidone and hachimycin, reflecting their unified therapeutic roles in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2751
retrieved_docs.content: Metronidazole is linked to ADH antagonists, indicating that it shares a broader pharmacological relationship with agents that oppose alcohol dehydrogenase activity. In addition, metronidazole functions as an inhibitor of protein synthesis, disrupting the production of essential proteins in susceptible microorganisms. This dual association underscores metronidazole’s relevance both in modulating pathways related to alcohol dehydrogenase and in exerting antimicrobial effects through inhibition of protein synthesis, which is important for understanding its therapeutic applications and potential drug interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: amoxicillin/clarithromycin/omeprazole, amoxicillin/clarithromycin/vonoprazan, as well as bismuth subcitrate/metronidazole/tetracycline and amoxicillin/omeprazole/rifabutin; the three azithromycin‑containing combinations are additionally classified as “Gastrointestinal Agents, Other.” All H. pylori regimens contain clarithromycin, a macrolide that provides essential antimicrobial activity, and the amoxicillin/omeprazole/rifabutin product explicitly includes rifabutin as an active component, linking antitubercular therapy with acid‑suppression in a single formulation. These hierarchical and combinatorial relationships support informed prescribing and optimized patient care across antibacterial, antitubercular, cephalosporin, cytotoxic, and gastrointestinal therapeutic domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: Metronidazole serves as the parent compound of a broad anti‑infective family that encompasses systemic antibacterial imidazole derivatives, gynecological anti‑infective imidazole derivatives, nitro‑imidazole agents active against amoebiasis and other protozoal infections, local anti‑infective preparations, and antiparasitic agents, reflecting its extensive anti‑protozoal and anti‑parasitic spectrum. ADH antagonists are linked to metronidazole, and the nitro‑imidazole antimicrobial furazolidone shares this relationship. Within the “Medicinal product categorized by disposition” hierarchy, metronidazole is grouped with a large set of imidazole‑ and triazole‑derived antifungals—including flutrimazole, terconazole, isoconazole, ornidazol, oxiconazole, sertaconazole, tioconazole, butoconazole, fenticonazole, omoconazole, efinaconazole, ekonazol, and others—as well as the arsenic‑based agent acetarsol; penicillinase‑sensitive penicillin lies outside this hierarchy. The “Preparations”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: Ornidazol and tinidazol are classified as imidazole derivatives that are used as systemic antibacterial agents. Both drugs share the imidazole chemical scaffold and are employed to treat infections throughout the body, reflecting their membership in the same pharmacological group of systemic antibacterials. This common classification suggests that ornidazol and tinidazol have similar mechanisms of action and therapeutic applications in managing bacterial infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2262
retrieved_docs.content: ADH antagonists, a class of drugs that block the action of antidiuretic hormone, are broadly related to metronidazole, an antimicrobial agent, within the larger context of pharmacologic categories. Metronidazole itself is a key component of the combination regimen bismuth subcitrate / metronidazole / tetracycline, which is employed as a triple‑therapy protocol for the eradication of Helicobacter pylori infection. This relationship highlights how metronidazole functions both as an individual therapeutic agent and as part of a multi‑drug treatment strategy, while also being linked to the broader drug class of ADH antagonists in discussions of medication classification and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 453
retrieved_docs.content: Imidazole derivatives that are used as systemic antibacterial agents are classified under the specific drugs metronidazole, ornidazole and tinidazole, indicating that these three agents share the imidazole core and systemic antibacterial activity. Nitroimidazole derivatives, which are antiprotozoal agents effective against amoebiasis and other protozoal infections, are likewise grouped with a broader set of individual drugs, including metronidazole, ornidazole, nimorazole, propenidazole, secnidazole and tinidazole, reflecting a common chemical scaffold and antiparasitic mechanism across these medications. The broader category of antiparasitic agents (“antiparazitární látky”) encompasses metronidazole and ornidazole as child members, further emphasizing their role in treating parasitic diseases. Together, these relationships illustrate that metronidazole, ornidazole, tinidazole, nimorazole, propenidazole and secnidazole are all members of the nitroimidazole class with antiprotozoal activity, while metronidazole, ornidazole and tinidazole also belong to the imidazole subclass with systemic antibacterial properties, highlighting the overlapping therapeutic uses and chemical classifications of these agents in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1919
retrieved_docs.content: Tetracyklin serves as a parent classification that encompasses both Tetracycline Antibiotics and the combination regimen of bismuth subcitrate, metronidazole, and tetracycline. By grouping these agents under the tetracyklin umbrella, it highlights their shared pharmacologic lineage and indicates that the multi‑drug therapy containing bismuth subcitrate, metronidazole, and tetracycline is considered a component of the broader tetracyklin category. This relationship aids clinicians in recognizing the common therapeutic framework linking individual tetracycline antibiotics with combination treatments that include tetracycline as a key element.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 33
retrieved_docs.content: Metronidazole is a nitroimidazole derivative that acts as an antiprotozoal agent against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases and also possesses systemic antibacterial activity; it is classified as a child of antiparasitic substances within the broader “medicinal product categorized by disposition” group, which also includes the aminoglycoside amikacin, penicillinase‑sensitive penicillins, the topical antifungal ekonazol, numerous imidazole and triazole antifungals (terconazole, butoconazole, fenticonazole, isoconazole, ornidazole, oxiconazole, sertaconazole, tioconazole, flutrimazole, omoconazole), and benzodiazepine antagonists. Imidazole‑derived subclasses comprise systemic antibacterials (ornidazole, tinidazole), gynecological anti‑infectives (azanidazole, butoconazole, propenidazole, sertaconazole, tioconazole, flutrimazole, omoconazole, oxiconazole, terconazole) and local anti‑infective agents (antiinfekční látky lokální) such as furazolidone, which is also an antitrichomonal

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 56
retrieved_docs.content: Metronidazole is a nitroimidazole derivative with systemic antibacterial, gynecological anti‑infective, antiprotozoal (e.g., amoebiasis) and local anti‑infective activities; it is classified among imidazole‑derived systemic antibacterials, imidazole‑derived gynecological anti‑infectives, nitroimidazole‑derived antiprotozoals, antiparasitic agents and local anti‑infectives. Nitro‑imidazole agents such as furazolidone (a nitrofuran antibiotic) share pharmacological relationships with metronidazole and with ADH antagonists, indicating overlapping mechanisms. Arsenic‑based gynecological anti‑infectives, exemplified by acetarsol, belong to the anti‑infective and antiseptic class that excludes corticosteroid combinations, a category also occupied by imidazole‑ and triazole‑derived gynecological anti‑infectives. Econazole is a topical antifungal that is simultaneously an imidazole‑ and triazole‑derived topical antifungal and an imidazole‑derived gynecological anti‑infective, illustrating dual
